title: Chapter 20: The Loophole
content: The waiting room at Whitfield, Crane & Associates is all glass and blonde wood and magazines nobody reads. Bloomberg Markets. The New Yorker. A glossy thing called Privilege that I'm pretty sure isn't ironic. I sit in a chair that costs more than my first apartment in Bushwick — I know this because I once looked up the price of furniture like this on a 3 AM doom scroll, back when doom scrolling still felt like something I chose to do instead of something that happened to me.

I'm early. Fourteen minutes early, which is the kind of thing you do when you're nervous about seeing your mother for the first time in two years. I can't feel the nervousness — thank you, Schedule C, Section 12(d), emotional milestone whatever-the-fuck — but I can see my hands shaking in my lap, so I know it's there. The knowledge without the sensation is its own specific kind of uncanny. Like watching surveillance footage of yourself. The girl in the chair is scared. The girl watching the footage is just noting the data point.

The receptionist is a woman named Priya with cheekbones you could use as letter openers and a headset that probably costs more than my current rent. She catches my eye and says, "Ms. Hart will see you now."
Ms. Hart. Not "your mother."
Evelyn's choice.

I stand. My knees are steady, which is a lie my body is telling the room. I follow Priya down a corridor of glass walls and blonde floors and the kind of ambient lighting that makes everyone look like they just closed a deal. The art on the walls is expensive and meaningless. Abstract stuff in muted tones. The visual equivalent of the word "accordingly."
The 34th floor. Corner office. Evelyn's office.
Priya knocks twice, opens the door, and announces me with the same tone she'd use for any 10 AM appointment. "Chloe Hart."
Not "your daughter."
Evelyn's choice. Again.

The office is what you'd expect from a woman who made partner at forty-one and never looked back. Corner, 34th floor, view of Central Park that makes the trees look like broccoli florets and the buildings like teeth. Bookshelves with leather-bound volumes she's never opened — I know this because the spines aren't cracked, because Evelyn Hart doesn't read physical books, because the leather-bound set is decoration, like the art, like the sculpture on her desk that I think is supposed to be justice but looks more like a woman mid-seizure.

Evelyn is behind her desk. She doesn't stand. She doesn't hug. She gestures to the chair across from her — a leather wingback that creaks when I sit, warm and butter-soft under my palms. Tactile luxury, the kind that's designed to make you forget you're being interrogated.
"You look thin."

That's her opening. Not "How are you." Not "It's been two years." Not "I saw your TikTok about the thing with the demon contract and I was worried." Just: you look thin. A statement of observed fact, delivered in the same tone she'd use to note a typo in a filing.
"I need you to read a contract."
Her eyebrow lifts. One millimeter. I've seen that millimeter move mountains in depositions.
"I'm an intellectual property attorney," she says. "Not a contract litigator."
"You're the only lawyer I trust."

A beat. The room does something. The air pressure changes, or maybe it's just the HVAC cycling. Evelyn's perfume — iris and old paper, the same scent she's worn since I was twelve — catches me off guard. It's a time machine disguised as a fragrance. For half a second I'm fourteen again, stealing her scarf to wear to school because it smelled like her, like safety, like the woman who never said the words but always signed the permission slips.
"Show me."

I pull my iPad from my bag and set it on the desk between us. The contract glows on the screen. All one hundred pages of it. Digital ink that rewrites itself when you're not looking, but right now it's static, frozen, presenting its best face like a date who knows you're checking their profile.
Evelyn picks up the iPad. She puts on reading glasses — new frames, I notice, thinner than the old ones, gunmetal instead of black — and starts to read.

She reads fast. Decades of parsing dense legal language have given her a scanning speed that would make a speed-reader weep. Her eyes move in that practiced zigzag, left-right, left-right, flipping pages with a flick of her finger. I can tell when she hits the interesting parts because her reading slows, her lips press together, her finger holds the page a beat too long.
She gets about ten pages in and stops.
She takes off her glasses. Rubs the bridge of her nose. A gesture I've seen a thousand times — Sunday mornings at the kitchen table, the New York Times crossword, her only willing vulnerability.
"Where did you get this?"
"I signed it."

She puts her glasses back on. She looks at me over the frames, and for one fraction of a second the mask slips. It's not concern, exactly. It's something sharper. Something that looks almost like fear, if Evelyn Hart were capable of fear, which she isn't, which she'd deny, which would be its own kind of lie.

She reads more. She reads faster. Her finger swipes the screen in a rhythm that accelerates like a heartbeat. The Central Park view behind her is all spring green and indifferent wealth. Somewhere in the building, someone is making a six-figure decision about stock options, and somewhere else, my mother is reading the terms of my soul.
Page 47. Section 12(d). Schedule C.
The emotional milestones.
Her face doesn't change. Her knuckles go white around the iPad.

I watch her read about the deductions. Joy: deducted. Grief: deducted, with a conversion rate I'm not going to think about right now. Attachment: deducted, subject to acceleration clauses and penalty provisions. She reads the words that describe, in legally precise language, the systematic extraction of everything that makes a person feel like a person.
She doesn't say anything. She just keeps reading.

She gets to the jurisdiction clause. I know the exact moment because her reading posture shifts — spine straighter, chin tilted, the posture she uses when she's found something she can use. Or something she can destroy.
"'Governing Law: Uniform Commercial Code, 1923 Edition. Jurisdiction: Infernal Court, Seventh Circle, Subsection iii.'"
She reads it aloud like she's reading a restaurant menu that's accidentally listed rat as an entrée. The contempt is architectural.
She puts the iPad down.

She looks at me for a long time. Not with motherly concern — I wouldn't know what to do with motherly concern from Evelyn Hart; it would be like seeing a fish walk. No, she looks at me with professional assessment. The same look she gives a problematic clause. The same look she gives a weakness in an opponent's filing. She's not seeing her daughter right now. She's seeing a case.
Then:
"This is the most poorly drafted contract I've ever seen."
I blink. "What?"

"The entire framework is archaic." She taps the iPad screen. "The jurisdiction clause is non-binding under current conflict-of-law statutes. The data extraction provisions violate at least four federal acts passed in the last two years — the Digital Consent Act, the Cognitive Liberty Statute, the Personal Data Sovereignty Act, and the Right of Digital Personhood Act." She pauses. Her voice is controlled but there's something underneath it, something seismic, something that reminds me of the way the ground feels right before it splits. "The emotional deductions constitute non-consensual identity modification under the Right of Digital Personhood Act. This contract isn't just unenforceable, Chloe. It's criminally non-compliant. If this were filed in any federal court in this country, it would be struck down before the ink dried."

The sparkling water the receptionist gave me tastes like minerals and obligation. I take another sip. Set the glass down. My hands have stopped shaking, which means the part of me that generates fear has also been deducted, or maybe it's just that hearing my mother say "criminally non-compliant" with that particular intonation does something to my nervous system that fear can't compete with.
"There's a catch," I say.
"There's always a catch."

"The contract self-repairs. Any legal challenge triggers a patch. It rewrites itself to close the loophole. And it operates outside normal legal channels — the Infernal Court isn't exactly on the federal docket."
Evelyn clicks her pen. Once. Twice. The sound is sharp, deliberate, a metronome marking time in a piece only she can hear.
"And?" she says.
"And Mephisto threatened accelerated deductions if I pursued legal action. He showed up at my apartment last night. He knows I'm here."

I expect her to react to the name — Mephisto, the thing from the contract, the entity that's been mining my emotional interior like a coal company in deregulated country. But Evelyn doesn't react to names. She reacts to arguments.

She takes notes. Her handwriting is precise and slanted, the same handwriting that used to sign my report cards, the same handwriting that wrote "well done" on a post-it when I got into college, the closest thing to praise she ever offered.
Then she says something I don't expect.
"The self-repair mechanism is actually a weakness, not a strength."
I stare at her. "Come again?"

"The contract can only patch within its own framework." She draws a square in the air with her pen — click — and then another square inside it. "Think of it like a building. If the foundation is wrong, every repair built on that foundation inherits the same structural flaw. You patch a crack, the crack reappears one floor up, because the load-bearing calculation was wrong from the start. The self-repair can only use the material it was given."
She clicks the pen again. Click. The sound is starting to feel like punctuation.
"You don't attack the patches. You attack the foundation."
"How?"

"Jurisdiction." She stands for the first time. Walks to the window. Central Park spreads below us like a terrarium. "The contract's jurisdiction clause says it can only be adjudicated in the Infernal Court. Seventh Circle, Subsection iii. That's the foundation — the entire framework assumes that court as the forum for dispute resolution. Every patch, every rewrite, every self-repair iteration operates within the assumption that this is the governing venue."
She turns around. She's smiling.

I have seen Evelyn Hart smile maybe seven times in my adult life. Once at my high school graduation, and she denied it. Once when she made partner, and she called it "satisfaction," not happiness. And now.

"But under the Federal Jurisdiction and Conflict of Law Statute," she says, and her voice has the cadence of a closing argument, the rhythm of a woman who has been waiting her whole career to say something this specific, "any contract affecting U.S. citizens on U.S. soil defaults to federal jurisdiction when the specified forum is—"
She lets the silence hold for exactly one beat.
"—fictitious."
The word lands in the room like a verdict.
"Fictitious," I repeat.

"The Infernal Court is not a recognized legal forum under any treaty, statute, or case law in the United States or any jurisdiction I'm aware of. A forum selection clause that designates a non-existent court is void ab initio. Void from the beginning. And if the forum clause is void, the contract's entire dispute resolution mechanism collapses. And if the dispute resolution mechanism collapses—"
"Every patch built on it collapses too."
"Now you're thinking like a lawyer."
She says it like a compliment. From Evelyn, it is one.

She walks back to the desk. Sits down. Clicks her pen — and I notice, because I notice everything now, because I can't feel anything so I've become a reader of surfaces, that her hands have stopped trembling. They were trembling before. I didn't notice when they started, but I notice when they stop. It happened when she found the angle. When she found the way in. Her hands steady because she has a case, and a case is something she knows how to hold.
"I'll take it," she says.
"You'll take it?"
"The case. I'll represent you."

I didn't expect it to be this fast. I thought I'd have to argue. I thought I'd have to beg, or explain, or do the emotional labor of translating my desperation into terms she'd understand. But she found the angle on her own. She read the contract and she found the crack in the foundation before I even pointed at it.

"Not because you're my daughter," she says, and her voice is very controlled, more controlled than usual, which is how I know it costs her something. "Because this contract is the most interesting legal puzzle I've encountered in thirty years of practice."
I believe about half of that.

The other half is sitting in the way she says "we" instead of "you" when she starts talking about filing strategy. "We'll need to establish standing." "We'll need to demonstrate jurisdiction." "We'll need to prove damages." We. We. We. The pronoun that means she's already in. The pronoun that means she's standing next to me instead of across from me, and maybe that's the closest Evelyn Hart gets to "I love you" — she moves the preposition. From "across" to "next to." From opponent to co-counsel.
She pushes a notepad across the desk. Yellow legal pad, lined, the same kind she's used since before I was born. It's the first thing she's offered me in two years.
"We'll need to file in the Southern District of New York. And we'll need evidence of the emotional extraction — can your friend provide technical documentation?"

She means Leo. She doesn't know Leo's name, but she knows there's a friend, because I said "we" too, back when I explained about the contract's architecture, and Evelyn filed that detail away like she files everything.
I nod.

My mother looks at me. Really looks, for the first time since I walked in. Not at the thinness, not at the shaking hands, not at the data points. At me. The assessment drops away, and for a half second — no, a quarter second, Evelyn doesn't do halves when she can do quarters — there's something in her eyes that isn't legal strategy.
"I'm not doing this because you're my daughter," she says. "I'm doing this because this contract is an abomination."
I believe about half of that.

The other half is love, translated into the only language Evelyn Hart actually speaks: law. Clause and rider, filing and precedent, the architecture of argument. She can't say "I love you" — the words literally do not exist in her vocabulary in that configuration. But she can say "I'll take the case." She can say "we." She can say "abomination" with her knuckles white and her hands finally steady and a yellow notepad pushed across the desk like a bridge.

I pick up the notepad. The paper is smooth under my fingertips. Expensive, like everything in this office, like every weapon Evelyn has ever wielded. I write my name at the top — Chloe Hart — and under it, in parentheses, the word that matters: (client).
The loophole is real. The case is possible. My mother, who has never once in my adult life been on my side, is on my side.
Now we just have to sue a demon in federal court.
And wait for Mephisto to find out.
